decisions.title: Intent-level surface for agents, primitives as escape hatch
Two SQLite stores — index.db (cache) and pql.db (state)
In-house migration runner, not golang-migrate or goose
Ticket source of truth is SQLite, not markdown
One combined skill, not separate query/plan skills
State machine for ticket status transitions
Clean version strings — just the number
plan export and plan import for versioned planning snapshots
No status transition enforcement in pql
Replication via per-table monthly SQL changelog files
Inline LWW guards plus content hash plus canonical row columns
Git lifecycle hook architecture for replication
No ALTER TABLE — schema lives in CREATE statements only

tickets.title: unify show-tree rendering across ticket and decision surfaces
tickets.description: Audit which surfaces build a ticket/decision join-tree (ancestors, decisions, blockers, children) and route them all through buildShowTree (introduced in v1.4.5 in internal/cli/ticket.go). Currently used by ticket show, ticket refine next, ticket refine write. Plan whatsnext / plan review use the older whatNextResult shape; decisions show may have a parallel pattern. Goal: one canonical helper, identical JSON output where the data overlaps, single place to tweak rendering.

tickets.title: route plan whatsnext and plan review through buildShowTree
tickets.description: Replace whatNextResult in internal/cli/plan.go with the ticketShowTree shape (or refactor whatNextResult to embed it). enrichTicket already returns the same ancestors/children/decisions data — collapse the two so plan whatsnext, plan review, ticket show, and ticket refine emit identical join-tree JSON for the same ticket. Keep the Message field for the no-actionable-tickets case.

tickets.title: align decisions show with the join-tree helper pattern
tickets.description: Audit internal/cli/decisions.go (decisions show with --with-refs / --with-tickets) for a parallel ad-hoc tree shape. If found, factor into a buildDecisionTree helper analogous to buildShowTree so decision rendering stays consistent across decisions show, decisions read, and any future surface (e.g. plan status drilldowns).

tickets.title: pre-commit hook misses bootstrap snapshot when pql-plan.json is untracked
tickets.description: internal/cli/init.go:374 pqlPreCommitHook gates 'git add' on 'git diff --quiet -- .pql/pql-plan.json'. For an untracked file, git diff returns 0 (no diff) so the negation is false and the file is never staged. Result: the first commit after 'pql init' silently fails to commit the planning snapshot. Reproducer: fresh repo, pql init, pql ticket new ..., git commit -m '...' — pql-plan.json stays untracked. Fix: also stage when the file exists but is untracked, e.g. test 'git ls-files --error-unmatch ... 2>/dev/null' or unconditionally 'git add -f .pql/pql-plan.json' if the file exists. Update both the embedded hook script and any tests asserting hook behaviour.

tickets.title: harden plan export hook: --stage flag + absolute pql path
tickets.description:
Two related issues with the pre-commit hook installed by pql init:

1. Staging logic lives in shell, not pql. Move it into 'pql plan export --stage' so pql owns the git-add edge cases (untracked / gitignored / dirty). Bare 'pql plan export' stays side-effect-free; --stage opts in. Update internal/cli/init.go's pqlPreCommitHook to call 'pql plan export --stage' (one line, no [ -f ] test).

2. Hook silently no-ops when pql isn't on PATH for git's hook shell (witnessed locally — git commit -m '...' didn't fire the hook even though pql was on the user's interactive PATH). Fix: at pql init time, resolve os.Executable() and bake the absolute path into the generated hook script instead of 'pql'. Drop the 'command -v pql' guard since the absolute path either exists or it doesn't.

Touches: internal/cli/init.go (hook content), internal/cli/plan.go (export verb adds --stage flag and stage helper). Requires a new repo-level helper or direct git shell-out for the staging step. Add tests: hook-content assertion that the resolved path is baked in; cli test for 'plan export --stage' both when file is untracked and when it's already tracked.

tickets.title: pql init writes hooks to .git/hooks/ even when core.hooksPath is redirected
tickets.description:
When git's core.hooksPath config points at a custom directory (this repo uses '.githooks/' for its pre-push gate), git ignores .git/hooks/ entirely — so the pql shim ensureGitHookShim writes there is never invoked, and the .pql/hooks/{pre-commit,post-merge} scripts never fire from git commit / git pull. Witnessed locally: the show-tree unification commit (T-2) didn't auto-stage .pql/pql-plan.json even though pql plan export --stage works correctly when run by hand.

Reproducer: 'git config core.hooksPath .githooks' in any pql-init'd repo. Commits stop carrying the snapshot.

Fix: ensureGitHookShim in internal/cli/init.go should resolve the effective hooks path. Two strategies:
  1. Read 'git config --get core.hooksPath' (or the equivalent env var GIT_HOOKS_PATH) and write the shim there. If the directory doesn't exist or isn't writable, surface a clear note in the init JSON output rather than silently planting a dead shim.
  2. Append to an existing pre-commit/post-merge shim in that directory rather than overwriting (so the project's own hook keeps working alongside pql's).

Test: assert ensurePlanExportHook with a pre-set core.hooksPath writes into the redirected dir, not .git/hooks/.

tickets.title: ship clean-house skill v1.0 as pql sub-skill
tickets.description: Add a clean-house skill to the pql installer that runs documentation-discipline passes over a DQR (Decisions / Questions / Rejected) markdown system. Claude is the runtime (no external script); the skill walks decisions/, runs the rule catalog, batches findings via AskUserQuestion, and applies fixes via pql queries + Edit. Pql remains a dumb data layer — gets new queries only when the skill names one.

tickets.title: draft clean-house SKILL.md + references/rules.md
tickets.description: Create internal/skill/clean-house/{SKILL.md,references/rules.md}. SKILL.md is the v1.0 integrated form (Claude as runtime; no Python stopgap framing). references/rules.md contains 6-8 starter rules with explicit Detection sections that name the pql query / file primitive each needs — that detection list reveals the pql gaps for follow-up tickets.

tickets.title: expose decision body heading anchors on pql decisions read
tickets.description: Add a 'headings: [{level, text, slug}]' array to the JSON returned by 'pql decisions read <id>'. The slug must follow the same convention used by markdown anchor links in the source (typically lowercase-hyphen — match the project's existing slugger; check whether one already lives in internal/index/parser/markdown for headings used elsewhere). Required for clean-house RULE-ANCHOR-DRIFT detection — without it, the skill cannot resolve cross-reference link targets to existing headings without re-parsing the body.

tickets.title: extend skill installer to ship clean-house as sub-skill
tickets.description: Refactor internal/skill/ so the installer can ship multiple skills. Today it embeds one SKILL.md from internal/skill/SKILL.md and writes to .claude/skills/pql/SKILL.md. Need to also embed internal/skill/clean-house/{SKILL.md,references/rules.md} and write to .claude/skills/clean-house/. The install lockfile (.pql-install.json) extends to track both skills' state. Updates: pql skill install, pql skill status, pql doctor, pql init's --with-skill flag (if relevant). Tests: skill install creates both directories; --force refreshes both; status correctly reports each independently.

tickets.title: auto-resolve skill scope: user-scope wins when present
tickets.description:
pql skill install (no --user flag) should auto-resolve which scope to operate on:

  - If ANY bundled skill is already installed at user-scope (~/.claude/skills/<name>/), treat user-scope as canonical for the whole suite. Install/update every bundled skill there. Do not touch project-scope.

  - If no user-scope install exists, install at project-scope (current behavior).

  - --user flag still forces user-scope unconditionally — only way to make the *initial* user-scope install.

When auto-resolving to user-scope, also tidy up any safely-removable project-scope install (state in {current, stale, missing}). Preserve project-scope if state is modified or unknown — the user has hand-edits, do not nuke them; surface a note instead.

This same resolution applies to:
  - pql skill status (no flag) — report effective scope
  - pql init skill step — install/update at the resolved scope
  - pql skill uninstall (no flag) — operate on project-scope only (do not surprise users by removing user-scope without --user). Note when user-scope exists.

pql doctor should report BOTH scopes so drift between them stays visible.

Add a Scope field on the per-skill JSON output (user | project) so callers can tell which install they are looking at.

tickets.title: Track conditions for D-1 revisit
tickets.description: D-1 commits to no vector retrieval but documents two AND-conditions for revisiting: (a) a specific class of agent task is shown, with examples, to fail on structured retrieval and succeed on semantic; AND (b) a pure-Go embedded vector store reaches tier-one quality. Either alone is insufficient. This ticket exists to surface the revisit gate in plan whatsnext / plan board so the trigger doesn't get forgotten if/when the conditions arrive. No work to do until both fire — the ticket stays in backlog as a sentinel.

tickets.title: tighten clean-house rules.md (catalog fixes)
tickets.description:
Three changes to internal/skill/clean-house/references/rules.md, surfaced by the first end-to-end clean-house run:

1. RULE-ANCHOR-DRIFT detection rewrite. Current logic checks anchor links against the referenced record's body headings (from pql decisions read), but anchor-only links resolve against the file's full heading set — including record-level '### D-N: …' headings stripped from the body. Replace detection with: read the source body's *file* (decisions/<file>), extract every ATX heading, build a file-level slug index, check anchor-only links against that. Cross-file anchors (path.md#slug) check the path's slug index.

2. RULE-DEAD-FILE-REFERENCE filtering. Detection produced 6/6 false positives this run: placeholder tokens (tickets/T-NNN.md, sessions/.../brief.md), package-relative refs (repo/tickets.go for the real internal/planning/repo/tickets.go), sibling-relative refs (questions.md from a body in decisions/, resolved against repo root). Add to detection: skip tokens containing 'T-NNN', '...', '<', '>'; resolve relative paths against the source file's directory before os.Stat; fallback to basename-match across the repo (find -name) before flagging.

3. Per-rule Finding ID format. Each rule entry needs a 'Finding ID:' field so the skip ledger can re-recognize the same finding across runs (precondition for the 3-strikes promote-prompt logic). Examples: RULE-ANCHOR-DRIFT → '<source-record>:<target-slug>'; RULE-SUNSET-WITHOUT-TICKET → '<record-id>:<phrase-hash-8>'; RULE-FILE-OVER-THRESHOLD → '<file-path>'.

tickets.title: tighten clean-house SKILL.md (procedure + state)
tickets.description:
Five changes to internal/skill/clean-house/SKILL.md, surfaced by the first end-to-end clean-house run:

1. Add step 1.5 'Probe conventions'. Read decisions/.clean-house.yaml if present; otherwise infer from the project: heading depth (## vs ###), backlink phrasing (Resolves: vs other), staleness threshold (default 60). Rules.md entries should consume these probed values instead of hardcoding. Also documents the .clean-house.yaml shape so projects can pin conventions explicitly.

2. In step 4 'Walk decisions/', filter records whose file_path begins with 'legacy/'. The 'What this skill does NOT do' bullet promises this but the procedure doesn't enforce it.

3. In step 6, define 'stop asking' globally: exits all remaining prompts in the run (mechanical batches and judgment findings alike); everything pending lands in the skip ledger as deferred; summary still emitted.

4. In step 7, define the 'show diff first' loop: print unified diff of the staged edits, then re-prompt with the same options minus 'show diff first' so the user can't loop forever.

5. Define the promotion-candidate history file. Output template promises 'RULE-X (5/7 runs)' but no storage is named. Either repurpose the skip ledger with per-run section headers (timestamped, plus 'fired' / 'skipped' counts per rule) or add a sibling .clean-house-history.md. Pick one; document the shape.

tickets.title: exempt stance-shaped decisions from coverage report
tickets.description:
pql decisions coverage flags every confirmed D without a linked T. For pql itself, ~13 of 14 confirmed decisions are founding/architectural stances (D-2 'intent-level surface', D-4 'consumer-agnostic core', D-5 'CLI-first, not MCP', etc.) — they document commitments, not roadmap items. A T per stance is ceremony.

Proposal: introduce a frontmatter field on D records, e.g. 'coverage: stance' (free-form values to grow later — 'roadmap', 'sentinel', 'stance', default 'roadmap'). 'pql decisions coverage' filters out non-roadmap by default; --include-stance shows the full set.

Touches: internal/planning/parser/decisions.go (parse new field), internal/planning/repo/decisions.go (Decision struct), internal/cli/decisions.go (filter + flag in coverage subcommand), the existing decisions/*.md (annotate D-2 through D-14 as 'coverage: stance' once the parser supports it). D-1 stays default ('roadmap') because it has T-12 tracking its revisit gate.

tickets.title: implement changelog-based replication for planning state
tickets.description: Umbrella story for the changelog-based replication design captured in D-15 (changelog layout), D-16 (inline LWW guards + hashing), D-17 (soft deletes), and D-18 (hook architecture). Replaces the single mutable pql-plan.json snapshot (D-13, superseded) with .pql/changelog/<table>/ directories of per-month SQL upsert files plus per-table schema files. Children cover the discrete implementation slices: schema/columns, soft deletes, export rewrite, import rewrite, rebuild, init updates, legacy migration, schema-migration generation, and the clide integration test scenario. The original design doc at pql-plan-replication.md is retired in favour of these records — delete or archive once children land.

tickets.title: add canonical row columns and content hash to all planning tables
tickets.description:
Forward migration on every planning table to add three columns: created_at (timestamp, set on INSERT), updated_at (timestamp, set on every write — used as the LWW key and the changelog month-bucketing key), and hash (TEXT, set to H(canonical(row except hash))). Plus a canonical_version column or schema-level marker so canonicalisation rule changes are detectable.

Implement canonical row serialisation in internal/planning/ — a fixed column-order projection with strict value formatting (NULL sentinel, integer vs float distinction, timestamp ISO format). Compute hash at write-finalisation in repo helpers (internal/planning/repo/*.go) on every INSERT/UPDATE so the column is always current.

Inline LWW guard for upserts is the SQL idiom: WHERE excluded.updated_at > target.updated_at OR (excluded.updated_at = target.updated_at AND excluded.hash > target.hash). Codify it in a helper used by both writers (D-16) and exporters (T-19).

Touches: internal/planning/schema.go (forward migration), internal/planning/repo/*.go (write paths), new internal/planning/canonical.go (canonicalisation + hash function). Tests: round-trip determinism (two replicas hash identical content to identical bytes), canonical_version bump triggers re-hash, NULL/edge-value coverage.

tickets.title: implement soft deletes via deleted_at column
tickets.description:
Forward migration adding deleted_at (timestamp, NULL = live) to every planning table. Route every existing DELETE through UPDATE ... SET deleted_at = ?. Update every read query in internal/planning/repo/*.go to filter WHERE deleted_at IS NULL by default; add an --include-deleted flag where the surface needs visibility into stubs (e.g. audit, recovery).

Stubs persist in pql.db and the changelog so other replicas know the deletion is intentional rather than missing — without this, replay against a fresh replica would re-INSERT the row. The changelog upsert for a deletion is just a regular line where deleted_at is non-null.

Touches: internal/planning/schema.go (migration), internal/planning/repo/*.go (every read query gains the filter; every delete becomes a soft-delete UPDATE), audit log entries for deletion events (recorded as today). Tests: round-trip soft delete / undelete, replay convergence (replica A deletes, replica B has never seen the row → replay creates the stub, then deletion replays into the stub).

Stub purge / retention is deferred to Q-10. This ticket lands the column and the read-path filter; it does not implement purge.

tickets.title: rewrite pql plan export to emit per-table monthly changelog files
tickets.description:
Replace the current JSON snapshot export (writes pql-plan.json) with the changelog format: emit SQL upsert lines into .pql/changelog/<table>/<YYYY-MM>.sql, one file per (table, month) combination based on the row's updated_at.

Export pipeline (internal/cli/plan.go + new internal/planning/changelog/):
1. Read last_export_marker from pql.db meta table (local-only, gitignored side of state).
2. SELECT * FROM <table> WHERE updated_at > last_export_marker, ordered by (updated_at, hash). Two-pass: emit rows whose updated_at falls in the previous month into <prev-month>.sql, then current month into <current-month>.sql. Late-arriving rows for older months are rare but possible after clock corrections / backdated imports — handle the bucket placement by row, not by export time.
3. Each emitted line is an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(<pk>) DO UPDATE SET ... WHERE excluded.updated_at > target.updated_at OR (excluded.updated_at = target.updated_at AND excluded.hash > target.hash). Use the canonical-projection helper from T-17 so the SQL line is byte-stable across replicas.
4. Append (don't rewrite — files are append-only by month).
5. Advance last_export_marker after successful write.

Stage written files via git add (the existing --stage flag pattern from T-5).

Touches: internal/cli/plan.go (export verb body), new internal/planning/changelog/exporter.go, internal/planning/repo/meta.go (marker storage). Tests: round-trip with T-20 import; same-millisecond rows get sorted deterministically by hash; previous-month bucket only touched when rows belong there.

Decisions still source from markdown, not changelog (D-8 preserved) — exporter skips the decisions table.

tickets.title: rewrite pql plan import to replay changelog directories
tickets.description:
Replace JSON import with changelog replay. Walk .pql/changelog/<table>/ directories in lexicographic file order so 0000-schema.sql and other zero-prefixed schema files run before year-prefixed data files. Replay every line that hasn't been seen since last_import_marker (tracked per-file via mtime or per-line via offset).

Import pipeline (internal/cli/plan.go + internal/planning/changelog/importer.go):
1. Read last_import_marker from pql.db meta.
2. For each .pql/changelog/<table>/ directory:
   a. Run files in lexicographic order, tracking position.
   b. Schema files apply via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS (idempotent).
   c. Data files: each line is an inline-guarded upsert; replay is idempotent because the WHERE clause from T-19 prevents stale lines from overwriting newer state.
3. Advance last_import_marker on success.

Idempotency is load-bearing: the same line replayed N times converges to the same result, regardless of starting state. This is the property that lets the hooks (D-18) be cheap and the import to fire from multiple paths (post-merge, pql plan rebuild) without coordination.

Touches: internal/cli/plan.go (import verb body), new internal/planning/changelog/importer.go, internal/planning/repo/meta.go. Tests: replay-twice convergence, replay-out-of-order convergence, schema_version mismatch handling (refuse and surface diagnostic), partial-replay resumption after interrupted run.

tickets.title: implement pql plan rebuild for branch-switch and rebase recovery
tickets.description:
New subcommand pql plan rebuild — drops planning tables and replays the full changelog from scratch. Used by post-checkout (branch switch) and post-rewrite (rebase) hooks because LWW-guarded incremental replay can only INSERT/UPDATE — it can't remove rows that exist on the previous branch but not the new one.

Rebuild pipeline (internal/cli/plan.go):
1. DELETE FROM each planning table (or DROP and rely on schema files to recreate — simpler).
2. Reset last_import_marker, last_export_marker.
3. Run the import flow from T-20 against the entire changelog.
4. Stdout: summary (tables rebuilt, rows replayed, time taken).
5. Stderr: progress diagnostics for hook visibility.

Surface as a first-class command, not just a hook callee — power users may invoke it for disaster recovery or after manual changelog edits. Document under 'Plan subcommands' in the skill.

Touches: internal/cli/plan.go (new rebuild verb), reuses the importer from T-20. Tests: rebuild-from-empty, rebuild-after-branch-with-deleted-rows (must not leak previous branch state), idempotent re-rebuild.

tickets.title: update pql init to install hooks, schema files, and gitattributes for replication
tickets.description:
Extend internal/cli/init.go to wire the full replication lifecycle on a fresh clone or an upgraded repo:

1. Create .pql/changelog/ and per-table subdirectories (.pql/changelog/tickets/, .pql/changelog/audit_log/, .pql/changelog/refs/, etc. — every planning table that participates in replication).
2. Write 0000-schema.sql into each <table>/ directory containing the CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS statement for that table. The migration runner (D-6) generates these from its current schema knowledge — reuse rather than duplicate.
3. Install four hooks (resolved absolute pql path, per the T-5 fix):
   - pre-commit: pql plan export --stage (existing path; just confirm the verb still does the right thing under the new exporter).
   - post-merge: pql plan import (silent if quick; default behaviour).
   - post-checkout: if [ "" = "1" ]; then echo 'rebuilding pql database...'; pql plan rebuild; fi
   - post-rewrite: echo 'rebuilding pql database after rewrite...'; pql plan rebuild
4. Write or amend .gitattributes with the line: .pql/changelog/*.sql merge=union
5. Stage all the above so the user's first commit lands the new layout.

Touches: internal/cli/init.go (most of the work), reuses migration runner for schema generation, depends on T-19/T-20/T-21 verb behaviour. Tests: fresh-init produces all expected files; re-init is idempotent; .gitattributes append doesn't duplicate the line if it's already present.

tickets.title: migrate legacy pql-plan.json into the changelog on first export
tickets.description:
Existing repos have committed pql-plan.json artifacts (per D-13, now superseded). On first pql plan export against an upgraded repo, detect the legacy file and convert it transparently:

1. Detect: .pql/changelog/ does not exist OR is empty AND pql-plan.json exists at the vault root.
2. Run pql plan import --legacy <pql-plan.json> to populate pql.db from the JSON snapshot (the existing import path is the migration tool).
3. Run pql plan export under the new format — this writes 0000-schema.sql and the initial data files into .pql/changelog/<table>/.
4. git rm pql-plan.json so the legacy artifact doesn't linger.
5. Stage the new changelog directory, the schema files, and the deletion of pql-plan.json — all part of the same pre-commit hook invocation.
6. Stdout: clear migration message so the user knows what happened in the next commit.

The legacy import path needs a flag (--legacy) so it doesn't conflict with the new replay-from-changelog import. After this migration commit lands, all future operations use the new format.

Touches: internal/cli/plan.go (legacy detection in export, --legacy flag on import), internal/cli/init.go (probably also runs the migration when init is run on an existing legacy repo). Tests: clide vault as the target — clone, run upgraded pql, observe transparent migration commit; verify subsequent flows use the new format.

tickets.title: integrate schema migrations with numbered changelog schema files
tickets.description:
Adapt the in-house migration runner (D-6) so schema migrations land in the changelog directories as numbered files rather than (or in addition to) inline Go SQL.

Naming convention: <NNNN>-schema-<slug>.sql in each .pql/changelog/<table>/. Lexicographic sort puts these before <YYYY-MM>.sql data files automatically. Each file is idempotent (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS, ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN guarded by introspection or a try-catch in pure SQLite, etc.).

Migration runner changes (internal/planning/schema.go):
- On migration: write the migration SQL to <next-N>-schema-<slug>.sql in each affected table directory, then apply it locally via the existing transaction-wrapped runner. Update schema_migrations table.
- On import: schema files are part of the lexicographic replay — they apply via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS / ALTER TABLE patterns and the schema_migrations table tracks what's been applied. Replicas pulling new schema files apply them on next import.
- Include canonical_version metadata in the schema files so canonicalisation rule changes (per T-17) are visible alongside structural migrations.

This keeps D-6's runner architecture (transactional, forward-only, in-house) but moves the migration artefact into the committed changelog so it travels with data and is replayable by any SQLite tool.

Touches: internal/planning/schema.go (migration emission + replay path), schema_migrations table interaction, internal/cli/init.go (T-22 generates the initial 0000-schema.sql via this same path). Tests: migration writes the file and applies it; replica pulls the file and applies via replay; conflicting concurrent migrations on different replicas converge cleanly.

tickets.title: multi-machine integration test scenario with clide
tickets.description:
Use the clide repo (postmeridiem/clide) as the integration test bed for the new replication flow. clide has the right shape: multi-machine, multi-branch, real concurrent edits, decisions sourced from markdown, custom git hooks integrating with pql's hook chain — which is exactly what produced the original pql-plan.json merge conflict that motivated this work.

Test protocol:
1. Migration test: clone clide at HEAD (which still has pql-plan.json), run upgraded pql, observe the transparent migration from T-23 — pql-plan.json removed, .pql/changelog/ populated, audit log preserved.
2. Concurrent-commit test: two clones, each makes a different ticket transition (one closes a bug, the other opens a feature ticket). Both push. Second push should fast-forward without conflict because per-table monthly files diverge cleanly. Pull on the now-merged main shows both transitions in the consolidated pql.db.
3. Concurrent same-ticket test: two clones edit T-XX.status to different values. Both push. Second push pulls and consolidates; LWW resolves to the later (commit-time, hash) edit. No data loss, no manual conflict resolution.
4. Branch-switch test: switch between branches with diverged ticket state. Confirm the post-checkout hook fires the visible rebuild and pql.db reflects only the current branch's state.
5. Decision-divergence test: edit a decisions/*.md on one machine, transition a ticket on another, both commit + push. Decisions section reflects the markdown edit (still markdown-sourced per D-8); ticket transition lands in the consolidated state.

clide accepts the rough edges from the original pql-plan.json conflict for one or two more rebase cycles in exchange for being the integration test bed. Do not patch clide with bridge remediations (no manual merge=union on the legacy file, no disabling the auto-export hook) — the friction is the signal that proves the new design is needed.

Touches: no pql code changes — this is the validation pass after T-17 through T-24 land. Outcome: a write-up of observed behaviour vs expected, plus any bug tickets the test scenario surfaces.

history.old_value:
pql skill install (no --user flag) should auto-resolve which scope to operate on:

  - If ANY bundled skill is already installed at user-scope (~/.claude/skills/<name>/), treat user-scope as canonical for the whole suite. Install/update every bundled skill there. Do not touch project-scope.

  - If no user-scope install exists, install at project-scope (current behavior).

  - --user flag still forces user-scope unconditionally — only way to make the *initial* user-scope install.

This same resolution applies to:
  - pql skill status (no flag) — report effective scope
  - pql init's skill step — install/update at the resolved scope
  - pql skill uninstall (no flag) — operate on project-scope only (don't surprise users by removing user-scope without --user). Add a note when user-scope exists.

pql doctor should report BOTH scopes so drift between them stays visible.

Add a Scope field on the per-skill JSON output ('user' | 'project') so callers can tell which install they're looking at.

After landing: drop the redundant project-scope install in this repo via 'pql skill uninstall' since user-scope is current.
history.new_value:
pql skill install (no --user flag) should auto-resolve which scope to operate on:

  - If ANY bundled skill is already installed at user-scope (~/.claude/skills/<name>/), treat user-scope as canonical for the whole suite. Install/update every bundled skill there. Do not touch project-scope.

  - If no user-scope install exists, install at project-scope (current behavior).

  - --user flag still forces user-scope unconditionally — only way to make the *initial* user-scope install.

When auto-resolving to user-scope, also tidy up any safely-removable project-scope install (state in {current, stale, missing}). Preserve project-scope if state is modified or unknown — the user has hand-edits, do not nuke them; surface a note instead.

This same resolution applies to:
  - pql skill status (no flag) — report effective scope
  - pql init skill step — install/update at the resolved scope
  - pql skill uninstall (no flag) — operate on project-scope only (do not surprise users by removing user-scope without --user). Note when user-scope exists.

pql doctor should report BOTH scopes so drift between them stays visible.

Add a Scope field on the per-skill JSON output (user | project) so callers can tell which install they are looking at.
